sentences.text: Well, we were in these wagons three days and three nights.
And we arrived at a place-- stopped at a place on the 10th of October, very early in the morning.
We looked through the little window up there, and we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saw a sign at the railway station, Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there was another sign, [NON-ENGLISH].
And we said, oh, we are in Poland.
It looks like Poland.
It looks like Poland.
Maybe here we-- we're going to start working.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The platform of the train was floodlit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw loads of SS men with dogs and whips and, you know, on-- on the platform.
And we also saw some people in a striped uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The doors were opened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the SS men were shouting, raus!
Raus!
Schnell!
Schnell!
And we were put into-- in front of each wagon in row of fives.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They selected men, women, and children to go to one side.
Oh, by the way, one loud speaker called out and said, look here, we are going to the camp, all of us.
You are going to work for the German Reich, all of you.
We're going to take the women, children, and the old people by bus to the camp.
And you-- the people who can walk, the young men, young men who can walk, will walk to the camp.
It's approximately a half an hour of-- of walking.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one woman, who begged an SS man to-- not to be-- she said, I'm young.
I can walk with my husband.
He said, no, you're going to go on that bus like all the other women.
And you will be a reunited in the camp with your husband.
She said, no, please, she begged.
She went on her knees.
Please, let me stay with my husband.
He hit her in the face.
She fell down.
And the husband ran towards her-- darling!
And he-- the SS man hit her-- hit him on the head and said, now you go.
And blood was coming off his head.
Now, you go with  your wife onto the truck together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's something that happened just in front of me, and it's another thing that I will also never forget.
We were hit by the SS to go stay in line.
And we were-- we marched [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the road, to a camp.
I think it took us only somewhere around 20, 25 minutes or so.
And from far away, we saw a big gate with a sign on it, 'Arbeit Macht Frei'-- work gives you freedom.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were in rows of fives, so and so many people to each group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was early in the morning.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We were put into a barrack where [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] people in striped uniform, inmates, were shaving our heads.
We were trying to find out from them where we are-- what's happening here.
Are we going to find our families and all that?
But in that room were big contingents of SS men.
They were-- they were whispering to us we are not allowed to talk.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were shaving our heads, our hair off.
Then another one came to tattoo our number on it.
The number that I have is 157103.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were given a-- we were told to go and be deloused.
We were rushed out.
It was already-- in October, it was already cold in Poland.
It was really bitterly cold.
And it was raining.

And we had to undress completely in one barrack and run out-- leave our clothes where they are, with shoes and everything, run out completely naked to another barrack, where there were showers and-- and where they sprayed us with-- brushed us with some chemical liquid, delousing us.
And we had a shower.
We were taken out of that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the shower, out in the cold again, out in the rain, naked.
We went to another block, another barrack, where we were given a pair of trousers, a striped-- all in striped clothes-- jackets, pajamas, and a shirt, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some wooden shoes.
They were-- the-- the sole was-- was not leather, but it was-- the sole was completely wooden, and the top was like canvas.
Not leather, but it was canvas or something.
Or plastic.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were asked to put it on.
We still didn't eat anything.
We still didn't-- you know, for three days and three nights, we did not eat.
I forgot to mention, naturally, in our wagon, there were two people who died.
That didn't-- they were carried out because they were dead when-- when we arrived in Auschwitz.
We were terribly hungry, and we were then allocated a barrack.
The barrack-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we saw as we entered the barrack, there was one separate room which was the home of the blockalteste.
It was the chief of the block, who was mostly not a Jew, but it was either a German criminal or a political prisoner or something, but not a Jew.
Very rarely was it a Jew.
It was the chief of the block.
We were allocated a bed.
Two people slept in one bunk.
And the bunk was only as-- as wide, I don't know. 36 inches or something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were given a blanket.
And I joined up with another Viennese fellow, young fellow my age, in one bunk.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Finally, they came with some hot water, coffee, and we were given a piece of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We had to go then to another barrack to register.
They took the number, took the name, and, you know, we had to answer questionnaires.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we went back into the barracks.
We were not allowed to touch the bed, to lie on it, because that's what the blockalteste told us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we were allocated-- allocated work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were told the next morning that we are starting to work.
We were put into groups-- in commandos.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we assembled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the appelplatz, which was the big square in the camp, in groups, all of us, we saw thousands and thousands of people in striped uniforms, like us, with their striped cap.
And a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a German criminal-- I don't know if you know, but the German criminals had a green triangle.
We Jews had a red and yellow triangle as Star of David.
The political prisoners had a red triangle-- some with F from France, or G from-- D from Deutschland, from Germany, and-- and political prisoners.
Anyhow, we went-- the one fellow came to us with a green triangle and said, I am your capo.
You're going to work under me.
And you will follow my instructions.
Whoever doesn't follow my instructions will be slaughtered, will be hit.
And I will see to it that you work.
And I am the-- I am your boss.
I am your-- your leader, and you have to follow.
You are going to work.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And we marched out-- out of the camp.
And we heard the music playing.
It was an orchestra.
As we marched out to work, we had to greet the SS by-- by putting our hats, caps off their head, because the capo shouted, mutzen auf!
Auf!
So we had to take the hat off in front of the SS and march out in even step.
And we walked from somewhere around 15 minutes and went to a very big factory where they-- where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were all these people in uniform, in striped uniform, working.
We saw them digging roads.
We saw them carrying a heavy metal thing on their back.
And we were told that we are the cement commando.
We had to carry 50 kilo cement bags from our-- from a train to a factory.
And we had to-- this is what we did all day long.
And if we didn't walk fast enough, we were hit.
And it-- it was something that a lot of people just could not stand.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sometimes the capo did hit us, especially when he saw some SS men coming.
Then he really showed-- wanted to show his authority and started whipping us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came lunchtime, we-- they brought a big bucket of soup, hot water out.
But it was very thin soup, nothing in it.
And we ate.
We stopped work for half an hour.
We sat maybe in a building and ate the soup.
And then we continued working.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when it-- just before it gets dark at night, we lined up five in a row.
The capo counted us, and we marched back through the gate.
Music again, march, a German march.
Again, mutzen up!
Greeting the SS.
And went to the barracks, and we got our-- another liter of soup.
This eating of the soup, we always try to be towards with the end of the queue, because in the big bucket, on the bottom, maybe vegetables were sitting.
But we were whipped into queue, to line up, because nobody wanted to be the very first one.
And we found, really, that our blockalteste, who was giving out the soup, really did it very good.
He-- he mixed it around, and-- and somehow or other, we did get, in the evening, a much better soup than we did at lunch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot to mention that in the morning, we had a coffee, or break-- ersatz, they called it.
Like liquid-- hot liquid and a piece of bread and a piece of margarine.
Sometimes we had-- once a week or twice a week, we had maybe a piece of sausage to go with.
So this was our diet.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was extremely lucky.
I told you that  I befriended a Dr.
Robert Weiss.
When we arrived in Auschwitz, I met him a few days later.
He said, oh, Freddy, how lovely [INAUDIBLE] to see you again this year.
You know, he embraced me.
You know.
He said, how are you?
I said, well, I'm very hungry.
He said, I'll tell you what.
I want you to know, I am in charge of the hospital in Auschwitz, in Buna/Monowitz.
That was our camp.
Our camp was Auschwitz Number Three.
It wasn't the main camp-- Auschwitz Number Three, Buna/Monowitz.
And I tell you, every night you come home from work, and you have your meal, come to the hospital, and I will give you some soup or whatever I can, because you have been my friend.
And I want-- want you-- you need that extra food.
And I believe that he saved my life because to have double ration, sometimes I had a piece of bread.
Sometimes I had another liter of soup.
It was really something.
You know, it was unbelievable to have double rations.
I'm pretty certain that this is what saved my life.
And he got the extra rations because it was a hospital that he was--
Yeah, and he was in charge of it.
Like the blockalteste is in charge of food for the whole of the barrack, he's in charge of the-- of the hospital for-- for the inmates.
And he-- he was the boss of the whole of the hospital.
He was a lovely fellow.
Robert Weiss.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we worked.
After the cement commando was put in to a different commander, they asked, who knows about Schlosseri?
What's Schlosseri?
It's locksmith or something.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I put up my hand.
I didn't know a  thing about it.
But I would-- we wanted to get away from the cement commando.
So we went there, and we had to, as a locksmith or something, we had to carry a heavy rail-- rail to put rails together, and metal parts from, again, a wagon into-- into the factory.
But most of the time, we were actually working indoors, in the factory, most of the time, which was fantastic because it was already-- we arrived in October.
I don't know whether you realize, but in Poland, in December, January, it was bitter cold.
That was very lucky because the capo of this new commando, who was a political prisoner, he was very good.
He said, look here.
I'm not going to rush you.
You can stand around.
We had a-- a-- a big bucket with hot coal and wood on the fire to warm yourself up.
You can stand around it.
But we need to have somebody watching out.
If the SS man comes, you're going to work like mad, and I'm going to shout at you [INAUDIBLE].
It was really a fantastic guy.
[SIGHS] I wish I remember his name also.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SCOFFS] Well, he was very good to us.
He was very good.
On one of the-- while I was in this commando, I heard from somebody that English prisoners are working also in the factory and very often, that they give away food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I said to the capo, look.
I want to go to the toilet.
He said, yeah, OK.
Come back quickly.
I went in the direction of the toilet, but I went towards the place where the English soldiers  were.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somehow or other, they were smoking.
They had-- they had Red Cross parcels.
And one of soldiers-- you know, I winked at him.
He was smoking, he was throwing away cigarette towards me, because they were guarded  by the SS men.
They were not allowed to speak to us.
I picked up the cigarette.
I didn't smoke.
I didn't want to smoke.
I-- I-- I put the cigarette in my pocket because I knew I could exchange the cigarette for bread when I go back into the camp, because some people wanted to smoke.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
On one of the trips, again, to the English contingent, I was stopped by an SS man.
Where are you going?
I said, oh, I'm going to the toilet.
He said, what do you mean, toilet?
This is over there.
Where are you going?
He said, how long have you been away?
Where is your commando?
He took me to a barrack.
He called in the capo, our capo, and he said to the SS man, well, this man has been away for quite a while.
We missed him.
And we looked out for him.
He's been away for quite a while.
OK.
[SIGHS]
As a punishment, I got 20 strokes on my bottom with a stick.
And my bottom was bleeding, and they carried me back into the camp.
And I was-- for three days, I was in the hospital with my friend, Robert Weiss, and I was taken care of there.
And after three days, I had to go out again and go to work.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Another thing that I will never forget that while we were working in the rain, and we returned to the camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] usually straight after the-- going  through the door, after taking our caps off, we'd go directly into the-- barracks to be fed.
But this time, they told us all line up in the usual place on the appelplatz.
The-- as we arrived at the appelplatz, to our horror, we see three black gallows in the center of the appelplatz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wow!
What's going to happen?
Who is going to hang?
What's-- we saw--
We all had to line up.
And, you know, we were about-- on the appelplatz, I think about-- it must-- I think we were 15,000 people in that-- in that camp.
Everybody had to line up.
Suddenly, the-- from the watchtower, the spotlights were showing on the black gallows.
And we heard SS boots coming, surrounding the appelplatz with machine guns.
And the blockalteste came with an SS officer.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the-- there was the camp commander, the notorious Schwartz was his name.
He was there.
And we said, wow!
What's-- what-- who are they going to do it to?
We didn't know, naturally, the answer.
The blockalteste went with an SS officer to the bunker.
They had a bunker there.
And they brought out three prisoners.
We knew-- the lights were shining on these three.
And we knew in the light who they were.
One was called Leon, Leon Diamond.
One was called Nathan.
I have forgotten the other name.
And the other one-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] these were three.
Leon Diamond-- all three were actually working in the SS barracks.
They were very prominent people, and we were very envious of them.
They were Jews.
Very envious of them because-- because of their position in the SS barracks, they were able to get more food than anybody else.
They never ran hungry.
But we heard, and we knew, that they were-- had, like, an underground movement-- part of an underground movement-- because they took away from the selections.
I will tell you about these selections later on.
They took people out of selections.
They were not gassed because they were too skinny.
Anyhow, to make a long story short, they came to the gallows.
And the SS officer shouted in the name of the German Reich, you know, because of activities against the German government, you are being condemned to die by hanging.
And they were put on the-- underneath the gallows.
And one shouted-- I think it was Leo Diamond-- shouted, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]!
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't worry comrades, we are the last ones.
It was already in 1944.
It must have been in October, actually, yes.
It was October 10.
It was one year we were in-- in-- 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The SS officer got red in his face and told to the hangman to put his noose, los!
And he went back.
The next one, I think it was Nathan, who shouted, [NON-ENGLISH   SPEECH]-- long live liberty.
And again, he was done.
Then the third one, he didn't say anything.
But we were so elated by this defiance of-- in-- they knew they were going to hang.
And their defiance, because never, ever has it happened that we shout, in the presence of SS officers, something in defiance to them.
We were all so elated.
And we-- like a mass of people, we shouted, goodbye, shalom!
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Goodbye!
You know, and to-- to-- For us to do that in front of the SS men, it was also quite a defiance.
But it affected us in such a way.
It was quite moving.
We were then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is something that I also remember very vividly, very vividly.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes.
I went-- I went to an interview with the-- and I told him I'm a cellist.
And they had cellos there.
And he asked me in [INAUDIBLE], he said, look here.
You have no chance, because we have here professors of music.
We have people who played in the German symphony orchestras.
He said, have you ever been a professional?
He  said, we are all professionals here.
We don't need anybody who is-- I said, look,  I read music, and this here.
And I tried to really persuade him.
But it was to no-- no way.
It would have been quite fantastic if I could have.
You said too you would tell me about the selections.
Well, yes.
We had from time to time-- we had to walk naked in front of doctors.
I don't know-- some people say Mengele was there.
I do not know.
I do not know.
I only know that we are to march in front of-- of SS officers in uniform, and then they looked at us naked and said, you go there, or you go there.
If people couldn't walk very well or could not-- had bones were showing, and they knew that this is the end of their strengths.
But we were always told, you must walk up very straight and marching, so that you should not be selected.
You must show them that you are OK, fit to work.
Because we knew that once you are being put on the side there, in that side, you are going to die, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew-- we-- we-- we-- people were talking about the chimneys.
We knew.
We heard about chimneys, although I must say, always from third persons.
It was never somebody who was there in person.
And somehow or other, if you feel that this can be maybe an imagination, maybe, there is always, if you're an optimist like I was, I am always-- I have always been an optimist.
And I felt I will live, and I must live, then-- and that there is-- being an optimist, I had hope.
I had hope to survive, and I wanted to survive.
I wanted to survive to be able to see my family again and tell them what has happened to me.
And it-- it was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this optimism that I have, I think kept me also alive very much.
And the-- the whole attitude, I knew that some people just could not-- did not have that attitude I had, and they did not last very long.
They were selected.
They collapsed.
Some of them, I remember, was-- I woke up.
In the next bunk of mine, there was an older man who died in-- at nighttime.
You know, he just didn't wake up anymore.
But I knew he gave up.
He always said, I know I'm going to die here, when we spoke to each other.
He always said, I know we'll never come out of here.
We are finished.
I mean, with an attitude like this, here, I feel that he talked himself into dying, I believe.
But with an attitude like mine, I think my attitude kept me alive, not only this, but also the double ration that I had from Robert Weiss.
And this is what we experienced in our-- in our barracks.
